transcript.text: What did you do at your mechanic?
I was their inaugural head of marketing, ran all content, community partnerships, you name it.
And actually, they had a quite large SEO program there.
So, yeah, it was really pretty incredible.
was like 20,000 pieces of content in 18 months.
that was when AI was a tool that people were using.
So, yeah.
That's cool.
Yeah, your mechanic was, they won a year before I joined.
I joined in 2013.
So, sorry, Marcel, how you doing?
We're just reminiscing.
That's awesome.
Like, how dare you guys already become best friends before you join the call?
Yeah.
We also have yellow water bottles.
Oh, yes.
Yeah, Marcel, you're already behind.
You're well behind.
Get back with the program.
It's the palette of the season, I think, this year.
Yeah, but, well, I guess you'd all just introduce yourself.
But Maddy, think a couple of things we wanted to kind of do.
One was just like kind of say hi, kind of get feedback and give you a little bit of context of how craziness the business has been evolving, kind of catch you up.
But then most importantly, also like just kind of hear feedback on how things are going and kind of learn from the process as much as possible.
Aaron ended up being an angel investor for us, which was like super exciting after our call.
And our call, I remember very well also because like a lot, like you validated the way you talked about a lot of things, validated so much about our approach.
But then also like we're growing so fast.
We're almost 50 customers and almost 50 people in the company, which is like almost triple when we had our first conversation.
It's insane, you know, but but because of that, obviously, like there's all the growing pains that as a CEO and, you know, now Matthews or content officer, it's like we want to make sure like we're.
Just, like, really being thoughtful and understanding, like, where can we improve?
Where can we, like, really knock it out of the park?
How can we guide the teens better?
How we can deliver things every week better?
And just try to, like, improve very quickly as well, you know?
And the only way to do that is to get feedback.
You know, you can't do it by just, like, you know?
I appreciate that.
I've had a lot of vendor relationships over my career, so I'm very comfortable giving feedback, and I'm pretty, you know, brutally honest when it comes to feedback.
So, you know, I figure if you're asking for it, then I'll give it to you.
Yeah, it's like, you're going to take it, but cool.
Okay, so let me just maybe, so you all did intros already, right?
We can skip that?
Or, okay, perfect.

I can give you just, like, a really high level of kind of how we're set up now, and then that will kind of better help you understand as you, like, as you, you might only interact with certain parts of it, right?
And so, it's...
Essentially, the way we're set up right now is we have Matthew as our chief content officer.

And part of the reason Daniel and I, my co-founder, we fell in with Matthew's approach is just how he became an editor was just breaking down amazing writing in first principles and just being this kind of reader.
Just everything about his story, I think, really resonated for how we go about.
I'm starting to call it atomic content, essentially, right?
Like breaking it down through the atoms and just like learning from it.
Then like what we started to set up is what we call managing director.
So think of the managing directors are like managing partners at a law firm, right?
Like depending on like your space or your area of expertise or whatnot, like a managing partner is like maybe selling.
It's bringing new customers.
It's hiring.
It's managing the team.
It's like managing a lot of the relationship with customers, right?
Bringing the strategy.
And sometimes doing a lot of the execution.
And so the managing directors, we brought in a ton of really top-notch people.
So like Dave built Fanatics and was head of SEO at TripAdvisor before that.
We have Jacob who built like G2.com, the entire content strategy there.
Like a bunch of like, Stuart was doing that for NerdWallet most recently.
And before that, like Nordstrom and Walmart.
So essentially we hire like heads of either content, SEO, growth, or a combination of those things as some of the brands that people that have essentially done it multiple times for multiple brands.
They're well known for their content, SEO, and growth, right?
And then underneath that, the people that you interact mostly with as well are the managing editors, right?
And that are guiding kind of the editorial quality, executing against the strategy, guiding the strategy as well, but then managing the content managers.
And then the content managers are, you know, two, three, four within those little pods that...

Support, you know, from like an SEO strategy, execution, staging content, starting to hire a lot more graphic designers as well as we build out more work streams around like creative, like turning written content to PDFs, into graphics, repurposing graphics, right?
Starting to do long form content, interview based content.
And so we're expanding our offerings and so like we're really beefing up the team to make sure like our goal is to have a one-to-one ratio at least, right?
So being super transparent on like people on what we call the delivery team to customers.
So like, and that's one of the reasons we raise money is to not have to try to run this service layer as like, you know, squeezing the margins and we've hired the best of the best, right?
And so anyways, I'll shut up here.
The only other thing I'll say is we broke down the company now into two buckets, if you will, or two organizations, the delivery and the build organization.
And then the build organization.
We're hiring amazing principal engineers.
We recently hired a new head of design that used to be the head of design at Grammarly.
So a lot of it is if you think about the service layer, the people in the delivery team are our customers on the build side, essentially.
And so by doing that, we're able to just accelerate the roadmap and deliver things really quickly.

But that's not to say that we can't do a lot of custom things for you, meaning if there are certain workflows, we've done podcasts to articles or certain repurpose, or one of our customers said, hey, we have this illustration style.
Is there any chance to create a workflow that will recreate this illustration style for a bunch of illustrations?
We did it, and it did really well.
And then now we can repurpose for other customers.
And so we'll do a better and better job of bringing some of those things we're doing for other customers, but also we will love additional ideas and things that would help essentially take...
More and more off your plate and deliver more, more value.
And I'll shut up now.
So that was the TLDR.
That's great.
I think the feedback that I have, all that's fantastic to me.

I'm really glad you're hiring graphic designers because in this space, like you really need an expert who knows graphic design to look at these designs that AI is cranking out and make sure that like, for example, one thing that we've noticed, the graphics can be pretty good, but the logo has to be applied later.
Like on export, right?
Like that's not something that the AI is getting correct and it's going to mess up the kerning or it's going mess up the coloring or whatever.
So export the image and then slap on the watermark or the logo or whatever.
So it's really precise, right?
Like things like that matter to us.
But I'm glad they're investing in graphic design.
I think that's sort of a gap that we've found is, you know, they're repurposing some of our Figma files and that's only going to get us so far.
Like at some point, you know, we're going to need, I think.
So my feedback is a few different facets.
One, let's just start with design and imagery in general.
My feedback has been that the blog posts are really thin on images.
And in some cases, we're letting them repurpose images that we've used in other blog posts because they're so relevant and we're getting by there.
But ideally, these are new images and it's not Elvis spending his time making them.
But until you get there, someone has to make them.
And right now, it's like we're sort of scraping the bottom of the barrel because you've got maybe three images because that's all that Elvis has time for.
And that's a bit disappointing.
And that might not be his core competency either.
So it's taking him longer, ideal, et cetera.
Yeah.
Also, I think the overarching theme is that the weekly meetings really would benefit from I...
Sort of first 10 minutes are report out.
Here's what we've done.
Here's the, you know, results of what we've done.
Here's a current snapshot of the traffic and the value of the traffic that we've driven.

Like when I've worked with SEO agencies in the past, we've got a spreadsheet and then every single month there's a report showing the traction, the gains, the losses, you know, on the top keywords that we're tracking.
So we've talked about the top keywords.
I don't know if the team is tracking them.
I don't really have visibility into that.
I know they're talking about Looker Studio.
I know they're talking about, you know, tracking these URLs that they've established for us with the new content.
But what I want to know is from a keyword basis, like, are we changing?
Are we ranking?
And are some of those other posts that we might be cross-linking to ranking better, et cetera, just a singular view into the new content alone as like myopic view, right?
Yeah.
So.
Like reporting and consistently delivering, you know, that, that readout, right?
And then saying, okay, does this change our strategy?
Or is there something we expected to have traction by now?
You know, what are we doing?
The, the second pillar is, you know, reviewing the Airtable pitches.
There's a lot of duplicates.
And I don't love going in there and seeing, you know, all these repetitive topics that, for example, I went in today, and it was like the best AI tools of 2023.
And I'm like, why is 2023 mentioned?
in 2024, like several times, like that was mentioned.
There's this like 20 different iterations of the best HVAC software tools for, you know, growing your business.
You know, I should not be the one who's like filtering through those.
That should be pre-filtered before it gets to me.
And then what ends up happening is I just see it as such a burdensome task that I end up getting delayed on doing it because I'm
Like, man, I know when I go in there, I'm going to need an hour to just, like, you know, clean up the house.
Or I just get really into providing the feedback because I'm worried that if I see 2023, I need to explain that, like, that needs to be part of the prompt, that, like, we shouldn't be doing that.
And in general, it's, like, really hard for me to keep track of what are we writing about and what am I approving.
And if I approve it, is it just going get written or is someone going to check that, okay, that's a duplicate topic, ignore Maddy's approval of that.
Right.
So it just feels a little bit discombobulated.
And then one of the things that we realized, and I gave Elvis the guidance on this, is that, like, it was just feeling really, like, generic tone.
So I asked for him to just build in a little bit more of, like, a friendly, clever, slightly joking, sarcastic tone.
Right.
And that worked really well, actually.
And that has made the posts a lot more personable.
More.
More.
More.
Interesting to read.

And that's something that maybe when you're onboarding folks, like, that would be a key question to ask of, you know, are we allowed to have some leverage there, some leeway to apply a certain tone that is appropriate for your brand, for your readers.
So that summarizes it to some extent.
mean, I think the programmatic stuff where, you know, we've been paying sort of double every single month, and we haven't really gotten into programmatic yet.
Jimmy and the team have been, you know, really receptive to my feedback, which is, you know, let's just get backlinks going.
And, you know, we're not going to pay for those, you guys can pull from the budget that we've been spending on programmatic, and we haven't, you know, actualized.
So, thankfully, that's gotten up and running.
That's high value.
I'm happy to have that sort of in-kind trade-off.
But in general, like, you know, the publishing is going fine.
You know, they
They were not always checking the links in the blog post.
So, you know, when you publish something, just make sure that all your links work and they don't go to 404s or, you know, silly mistakes like that.
So I think there's just a little bit of a rebalancing to some extent of what are the really important human elements and what are the important AI content generation elements.
And then, you know, how does graphic design fit in?
How does, you know, Elvis' skill set match what's required of him?
Um, so, you know, that it just seems like either maybe he's really spread thin or there's some skill sets that are not, you know, always perfectly applicable to the job that he's doing.

Um, but in general, like we, one of the things that I'm concerned about is that we're producing content that is, um, so granular in each topic, but then repetitive that it's sort of like from 20 years ago, like the approach that we would take on SEO, right?
And now it's like, you really want to

Get as close as possible to, you know, high visual content, high structure content, not just blocks of paragraphs, but, you know, images woven in there, like, you know, cross-linking to reputable resources and, you know, numbered, bulleted lists, headings, like, you know, all of those things that make for a more engaging read because Google is really ranking on those factors.
So those are the most salient things that are coming to mind for me right now.
It's too early to tell, like, what the impact is going to be, but we are starting to talk about programmatic and the guys are going to deliver the wireframe, I guess, next week.
And I am hesitant to, you know, have any expectations formed around that just because it's been a bit of a C plus, B minus so far.
Yeah, yeah.
That's super helpful feedback.
I think, like, just to kind of play back on a few things that kind of stood out to me, Ray, like, at the end of the day, like, we need to be taking a lot more burden off the plates.
And in full transparency, it's one of the reasons why we're, like, going insanely aggressive on the content manager layer.
Right.
Because right now what's happening is, like, we grew so fast.
And that's not on you, obviously.

Like, that's why we're also having these sessions is because, like, we're really, you know, and bringing in Matthew and others, right, is, like, essentially our ratios got out of whack a little bit, right?
Like, and we need to have way, way more support for the managing editors.
And we need to have the directors now, which was the layer that pretty much didn't exist and Kyle was spread so thin, right, to lead the strategy more and make sure, like, the strategy is there.
So, in other words, like, the director should be owning the relationship, the strategy, and kind of the direction and guiding it, right?
And being a true, true expert, which they are, and I can vouch for that, and that's going to change very quickly.
And then the Elvis layer, right, should be ensuring quality at all times with an execution layer underneath them.
And that's where we missed, and, you know, that's on me, right?
And, like, we want to change that C- to an A+, ASAP, and you have, like, my full commitment, and I really appreciate the feedback because it's, like, it's spot on.

Like, like, 100% of what you mentioned, like, we should be doing way, way better, and I want to try to find ways to also, like, just try to deliver more value in the meantime to make up for that because that's not okay, you know, honestly.
And, you know, and whatever we can do, if there's anything, like, on your end that's, like, hey, there's this one thing that we're doing, like, you guys could take this on.
This would be super helpful, like, anything at all.
Like, we're super game to, like, go above and beyond for you.
Honestly, the backlinks and the graphic design, I think.
You know, if we could get some support on that front, because the graphic design, like if I have to pay for that, that adds up a lot, right?
It's be like 100% increase on like what we're paying you guys a month is just to have graphic design paired with that from our resources, right?
So that's something as you get those resources in place, I would love to see that take shape.
But immediately, I think the backlinks are the biggest, you know, value that you can add that is hopefully not an expense to you, but a big impact for us, right?
Yeah, that's perfect.
Cool.

And I'll check in with the team, but perhaps what we could do is just like schedule, maybe not next week or the following, just like if we haven't already, Matthew, for Smith.ai, it's just kind of like a reset session, essentially, right?
Like go through presenting a little bit of our plan going forward, like a little bit of calibration on the strategy.
Matthew and team can kind of come back also with like a little bit of recalibration on tone and voice and the uniqueness of like kind of how the content can be.
Right.
Like and we can present a couple of ideas on the design aspects as well.
Obviously, we'll keep executing.

So I think immediately what we'll do is make sure that if we don't have like at least a freelance designer readily available to support the account on our end, like that will make sure we'll get that as quickly as humanly possible.
We should have already some available with capacity that we can increase right away.
In addition to obviously like we're hiring like fast and furious to make sure like we we have the people overseeing that function as well.
Right.
Like and and then I'll take a look as well and just be personally involved.
But Matthew, is there anything else on your end, too, that would be helpful or anything that comes to mind or questions?
No, I'm going to do a content review of all the items that we publish.

From you or for you so far, because I do want to, I want to observe the tone, you know, firsthand and kind of examine that and the delta between what we were producing and, you know, what you've been more happy with recently.
I think that's, that's an integral part of our new onboarding flow is talking to clients about their voice.
And the voice thing is hard to calibrate unless you have, you know, done voice work before.
And I think, unfortunately, like SEO work tends to, and content work tends to, like, cue towards neutral.
And that does not always stand out.

And my goal is for our content to be, you know, missing readable, obviously, as necessary, especially given, you know, the prevalence of AI and search engines and how they're eating some market share there.
But more importantly, like human readable, like we, I don't want any of, any of the work that we produce for you to be good in one and not the other.
You know, I want people to be able to.
The page and kind of get that mouthfeel of veracity about your brand and about that you know what you're talking about and that perhaps it's somebody they like to work with or partner with.
And, you know, that's important to me.

So part of my I've been here a few weeks, but part of my next few weeks is content auditing from the ground up on all the clients, because I definitely want to make sure that we have a good feedback loop from this feedback is great.
I took notes on all of this and I think all of these points are valid, some of which we already were aware of in other clients spaces.
So it's really good to have the reinforcement and you're like, hey, you know, we need to we need to bump the priority and then other ones that are new and maybe even unique to you.
But it doesn't mean they won't be applicable to other clients.
So I'm really happy to have the feedback always.
Thanks.
There's a couple of things that I want to mention just before I forget.
So, um, AI search is really something that we're seeing a ton of leads start to come through.
Uh, it was like zero in Q4 for us and it was.
22 new clients in Q1.
So like a big difference.
And we're going to see that trend continue.
And that also goes back to the structure.
So I really favor high utility articles.
So if you're going to tell me it's a list of the top 10 AI tools, you better have criteria for how they met the top 10.
And it shouldn't just be a blurb about the 1, 2, 3, 10, right?
Like, tell me about pricing, cost, features, reviews, like, roll it up for me and tell me like how this summarizes to become the number one, right?
And thankfully, Smith.ai is really exceptionally well reviewed.
And if we're part of that top 10, because it's topical, then, you know, we meet the criteria, right?
But when it comes to the AI search, think, you know, reinforcing that message is something that it tends to pick up on.
So if we keep saying Smith.ai is the best at X, Y, or Z, I start to see that in AI search results in ChatGPT, for example, right?
And that's going to happen in the instant results in Google, et cetera.
So I view it as a play on both.
I want to be showing up in AI search.
We already do that, but it's going to be really competitive.
And I want to continue to rank.
But the third pillar is, like, I want content that I'm not, like, embarrassed to have my name on it as the author and that I could tell my sales team, you know, this is a resource for you.
Like, it can't just be generic SEO content.
It has to actually be functionally appropriate and useful for the leader.
And that's also obviously going to help with ranking.
So if it's worth doing, it's worth doing right.
Yeah.
Yeah.
A hundred percent.
And I think, like, one thing that we're addressing right away, too, that I think will go to that point.
I just want to show you, like, this is the process to run for you, right?
And this is, like, one of the later processes, the steps is, like, what we call it.
of

fanatic you're I'm fixing that.

love that.

love it.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Gather feedback on GrowthX.ai's services and discuss improvements to content strategy and delivery for Smith.ai.](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX.ai has grown rapidly (50 customers, 50 employees) and is restructuring teams to improve service quality](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [Smith.ai identified areas for improvement: image creation, reporting, content filtering, and maintaining brand voice](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=425.0)
  - [GrowthX.ai commits to addressing concerns, particularly by expanding graphic design capabilities and backlink services](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=978.0)
  - [Both parties agree on the importance of creating high-utility content optimized for both AI and human readers](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=819.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX.ai Company Update

  - [Nearly tripled in size since initial conversation with Smith.ai](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [Restructured into "delivery" and "build" organizations](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [Hired high-profile talent (e.g., former Grammarly head of design)](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [Focusing on 1:1 ratio of delivery team to customers](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [Expanding services: long-form content, interview-based content, custom workflows](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)

### Content Quality and Process Improvements

  - [Smith.ai feedback:](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=425.0)
      - [Blog posts lack sufficient images (3 per post currently)](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=431.0)
      - [Airtable pitch process needs refinement (many duplicates, outdated topics)](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=621.0)
      - [Generic tone initially; improved with guidance for friendlier, clever style](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=715.0)
      - [Concerns about granular, repetitive content approach](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=819.0)
  - [GrowthX.ai response:](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
      - [Hiring more graphic designers](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=431.0)
      - [Implementing "polishing instructions" to maintain brand voice](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=1436.0)
      - [Matthew (CCO) to conduct content audit for all clients](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=1205.0)
      - [Commitment to high-utility articles with clear criteria and structure](https://fathom.video/share/J3cpnpvrykn9o3tpoeynsqQ5X3izW18i?tab=summary&timestamp=1269.0)

### Reporting and Strategy
